text: 17 organic acid–base complexes have been investigated with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), $^{15}$N solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (ssNMR) spectroscopy, and X-ray diffraction (XRD). In the solid-state, several nitrogen sites work as Brønsted or hydrogen-bond acceptors, and their detailed local structure can be disclosed by identifying protonated species as salts and hydrogen-bonded species as co-crystals by N 1s XPS, the large low-frequency $^{15}$N NMR shielding effect due to proton transfer, and minor changes except for a few large shifts due to very strong hydrogen bonds in NMR spectra of co-crystals. By combining the experimental NMR results with the Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations using CASTEP, excellent correlations between the calculated and experimental $^{15}$N isotropic chemical shifts are achieved and the structures are identified via NMR crystallography. Combining all these complementary results shows a powerful approach to disclose local structure and determine the state of proton or hydrogen bond in acid–base complexes. The approach is universal and especially for disordered or amorphous solids for which single-crystal XRD is not available. Hence, the structural information provided with this study has high relevance for pharmaceutical and materials chemistry.
